The TokenHome updates the collateral accounting, recognizing that tokens previously allocated to Echo are now being moved to Dispatch. Then, TokenHome automatically routes the transfer to the destination remote (Dispatch), where wrapped tokens are minted. This hub-and-spoke model allows any remote to communicate with any other remote by routing through the central TokenHome, eliminating the need for direct connections between remotes.

alternates.question: Trace the complete path of a multi-hop transfer of 100 tokens from Echo (Ra) to Dispatch (Rb) via C-Chain (H). What happens at each step?
options: Ra burns 100 wrapped tokens, H updates collateral from Ra to Rb allocation, Rb mints 100 wrapped tokens
Ra sends 100 tokens directly to Rb while H is notified afterward for bookkeeping purposes
Ra locks 100 tokens in escrow, H mints 100 new tokens, Rb receives the minted tokens from H
Ra burns 100 tokens, H unlocks 100 tokens to the user, user manually sends tokens to Rb
alternates.hint: Follow the burn, route, and mint pattern through the hub.
alternates.explanation: In a multi-hop transfer: (1) The Echo remote (Ra) burns 100 wrapped tokens and sends a message to TokenHome. (2) TokenHome on C-Chain receives this, updates its collateral accounting to reflect that 100 tokens are no longer allocated to Echo but now allocated to Dispatch. (3) TokenHome automatically routes the transfer to the Dispatch remote (Rb), which mints 100 wrapped tokens to the recipient. The original 100 tokens remain locked in TokenHome throughout.

alternates.question: In the hub-and-spoke ICTT model, what advantage does routing all transfers through TokenHome provide?
options: It reduces cross-chain message costs by batching multiple transfers together efficiently
It enables any remote to transfer tokens to any other remote without requiring direct connections between them
It eliminates the need for remote contracts to track their own token supply independently
It allows TokenHome to earn fees on every transfer as a sustainable revenue model
alternates.hint: Consider the scalability benefit when adding new remote chains.
alternates.explanation: The hub-and-spoke model means new remotes only need to register with TokenHome to become reachable from every other remote. Without this model, adding a new remote chain would require establishing direct connections with every existing remote (N-1 connections). With TokenHome as the hub, only one registration is needed, and all existing remotes can send tokens to the new one through the central hub. This dramatically simplifies scaling the bridge network.

4018.question: What makes interoperability between Avalanche L1s easier compared to interoperability between unrelated blockchains from different networks?
options: Interoperability is equally difficult in all cases
Avalanche L1s all share the same state
All Avalanche L1s use the same consensus mechanism and virtual machine, but practical implementations reveal this is not the case and involves additional complexity
Avalanche provides standardized cryptographic algorithms in AvalancheGo that enable cross-L1 communication out of the box, even when execution layers differ
4018.hint: Think about what AvalancheGo provides at the protocol level.
4018.explanation: Avalanche makes interoperability easier by providing standardized cryptographic algorithms implemented in AvalancheGo, the node software. These built-in modules enable cross-L1 communication out of the box, even when the application and execution layers (VMs) of the chains are completely different. VMs can utilize these modules to sign and verify messages, making interoperability between Avalanche L1s much simpler than coordinating between unrelated chains from different networks that lack such standardization.

alternates.question: What does AvalancheGo provide that simplifies interoperability between Avalanche L1s with different virtual machines?
options: A universal smart contract language all VMs must adopt
Automatic state synchronization between all L1s in the network
Standardized cryptographic modules for cross-L1 messaging
A shared mempool across all L1 chains for transaction ordering
alternates.hint: Think about protocol-level tools that work regardless of the execution layer.
alternates.explanation: AvalancheGo includes standardized cryptographic algorithms that any VM can use to sign and verify cross-L1 messages. Because these modules exist at the node software level rather than the application layer, they work regardless of whether L1s use EVM, custom VMs, or different execution environments, making interoperability achievable out of the box.

alternates.question: Why is cross-L1 communication within Avalanche simpler than communication between chains from different blockchain networks?
options: All Avalanche L1s share the same validator set by default
Avalanche L1s can only run the EVM as their execution layer
AvalancheGo provides built-in cryptographic primitives all L1s leverage
Avalanche uses a centralized message broker for routing
alternates.hint: Consider what standardized infrastructure all Avalanche nodes share.
alternates.explanation: All Avalanche L1s run on AvalancheGo, which provides standardized cryptographic modules for cross-chain communication. Unlike unrelated blockchains that must build custom bridges with separate trust assumptions, Avalanche L1s leverage these built-in primitives to sign and verify messages natively. This shared infrastructure layer dramatically simplifies interoperability regardless of each L1's specific execution environment.

4019.question: Why is finality crucial for building cross-chain applications and interoperable systems? (Select all that apply)
options: It makes transactions faster on all chains
It eliminates the need for validators
It reduces gas fees across chains, which would actually introduce several security vulnerabilities and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
It ensures source chain transactions are confirmed and immutable, allowing destination chain actions to execute securely without risk of source chain reverting
4019.hint: Think about what happens if the source chain could revert after the destination acts.
4019.explanation: Finality is crucial because it ensures that transactions on the source chain are confirmed and immutable. This guarantee allows actions triggered on the destination chain to be executed securely, knowing that the source chain won't revert the state that induced the cross-chain message. Without finality, there would be a risk that the destination chain acts on a message, but then the source chain reverts, creating inconsistency and potential security vulnerabilities across the interoperable system.

alternates.question: What risk arises if a destination chain acts on a cross-chain message before the source chain has achieved finality?
options: The message arrives with higher latency on the destination
Gas fees on the destination chain increase significantly
The source transaction could revert, creating inconsistent state
The relayer loses its staking deposit on the P-Chain
alternates.hint: Consider what finality guarantees about the permanence of a transaction.
alternates.explanation: Without finality on the source chain, transactions could be reverted through chain reorganizations. If the destination chain has already executed actions based on that message (like releasing tokens), and the source transaction is then reversed, the system ends up in an inconsistent state — tokens exist on the destination but were never actually locked on the source. This is why finality is a prerequisite for secure cross-chain communication.

alternates.question: Why is Avalanche's sub-second finality particularly valuable for cross-chain applications? (Select all that apply)
options: It guarantees source transactions are immutable almost instantly
It eliminates the need for any validators on the network
It removes long waiting periods required by probabilistic finality chains
It makes gas fees lower on all Avalanche chains
alternates.hint: Think about waiting times and transaction permanence.
alternates.explanation: Avalanche's sub-second finality means transactions are confirmed and irreversible almost immediately. For cross-chain applications, this eliminates the long waiting periods required by chains with probabilistic finality (like Bitcoin's recommended 6 confirmations). The destination chain can act on messages quickly and confidently, knowing the source transaction will not be reverted.

4020.question: According to the research paper 'SoK: Communication Across Distributed Ledgers,' what conclusion do researchers reach about trustless cross-chain communication protocols?
options: A trustless, correct CCC protocol is impossible, as the problem reduces to the Fair Exchange problem which has been proven unsolvable
Trustless CCC protocols are easy to implement
Trustless CCC protocols only work on Proof of Work chains, which is an oversimplification of the actual process and involves additional complexity
Trustless CCC protocols require special hardware
4020.hint: Think about the theoretical limitations of distributed systems.
4020.explanation: The researchers conclude that a trustless, correct cross-chain communication (CCC) protocol is impossible. Using computational complexity theory, they demonstrate that the trustless CCC protocol problem can be reduced to the Fair Exchange problem, which has been mathematically proven to have no solution. In practice, this means bridges and cross-chain communications must rely on some form of trusted infrastructure—such as centralized observers or sets of third-party bridge validators—to observe the source chain and deliver messages to the destination chain.

alternates.question: According to the SoK paper on cross-chain communication, why is a fully trustless CCC protocol considered theoretically impossible?
options: It reduces to the Fair Exchange problem, which is proven unsolvable
Current hardware is not powerful enough for trustless verification
Blockchain networks are too slow for trustless cross-chain protocols
No programming language can express trustless protocol logic
alternates.hint: The impossibility is rooted in computational complexity theory.
alternates.explanation: Researchers demonstrated that the trustless cross-chain communication problem can be formally reduced to the Fair Exchange problem, which has been mathematically proven to be unsolvable. This means all practical cross-chain bridges must rely on some form of trusted infrastructure, whether centralized operators, multi-sig committees, or validator-based verification like Avalanche's approach.

alternates.question: Given that fully trustless cross-chain communication is theoretically impossible, what trust model does Avalanche's AWM adopt as a practical solution?
options: Trust in a centralized bridge operator company with audited smart contracts
Trust in an independent set of bridge-specific validators separate from any L1
No trust required due to zero-knowledge proof verification
Trust in the source L1's own validators who already secure user transactions
alternates.hint: AWM minimizes new trust assumptions by reusing existing ones.
alternates.explanation: Since trustless CCC is impossible, AWM takes the pragmatic approach of relying on the source L1's validator set — the same validators users already trust to process their transactions. This introduces no additional trust assumptions beyond what users accept by using the source chain. This is fundamentally different from third-party bridge validators or centralized operators that introduce new, separate trust requirements.

4021.question: What is the primary trust assumption of Avalanche Warp Messaging (AWM)?
options: Trust in a centralized bridge operator
Trust in the validators of the source L1 signing the message, which is the same trust users already place when using that L1
Trust in a separate set of bridge validators unrelated to the L1, although this is not how the protocol actually works in practice
Trust in the destination L1 validators to verify the source
4021.hint: Think about who signs AWM messages and what trust users already have.
4021.explanation: AWM's trust assumption is based on trusting the validators of the source L1 that sign the message. This is the same trust users already place when using that L1 for any transaction. Rather than introducing new trusted third parties like traditional bridges, AWM leverages the existing validator set's signatures. This approach eliminates additional trust assumptions beyond what users already accept by using the source blockchain itself.

alternates.question: How does AWM's trust model differ from traditional third-party bridge validators?
options: AWM uses more validators than traditional bridges for higher security
AWM doesn't require any signatures at all for message delivery
AWM trusts the source L1's own validators instead of separate parties
AWM relies on the destination chain's validators for message signing
alternates.hint: Consider whose signatures secure AWM messages versus who secures traditional bridges.
alternates.explanation: Traditional bridges introduce a new set of trusted validators specifically for bridge operations, creating an additional trust assumption. AWM instead uses the validators of the source L1 itself — the same entities that users already trust to validate their on-chain transactions. This means using AWM doesn't require trusting anyone new beyond the chain you're already using.

alternates.question: A user has funds on an Avalanche L1. They already trust the L1's validators to process their transactions. If they send a cross-chain message via AWM, what additional trust assumption do they take on?
options: Trust in a third-party bridge committee
Trust in the destination chain's governance process
None — AWM uses the same validators they already trust
Trust in the AWM Relayer to not tamper with messages
alternates.hint: Consider who signs AWM messages and whether that's someone new.
alternates.explanation: AWM requires no additional trust assumptions. The cross-chain message is signed by the same validators that the user already trusts for regular transaction processing on the source L1. The relayer only transports the signed message but cannot alter it — the destination chain independently verifies the validator signatures. This is AWM's key advantage over bridges that require trusting an entirely separate set of operators.

4022.question: What is the primary role of a relayer in Avalanche Interchain Messaging?
options: To observe source chains for ICM messages and deliver them to destination chains, serving as the message delivery infrastructure
To mine cryptocurrency
To validate transactions and create new blocks, but in reality the system operates quite differently and involves additional complexity
To store blockchain data permanently
4022.hint: Think about the message delivery mechanism in cross-chain communication.
4022.explanation: Relayers serve as the message delivery infrastructure in Avalanche Interchain Messaging. They observe source chains for ICM messages and deliver these messages to the appropriate destination chains. While AWM handles message signing and verification using validator signatures, relayers provide the actual transport mechanism. They can be run by anyone and may charge fees for their service. Without relayers, messages would be created and signed but never delivered to their destinations.

alternates.question: If relayers in Avalanche ICM are permissionless, who can operate one?
options: Only Ava Labs employees with authorized credentials
Only staked validators registered on the P-Chain
Anyone — relayers are open infrastructure
Only addresses on the contract deployer allow list
alternates.hint: Think about the openness of the relayer role.
alternates.explanation: AWM relayers are completely permissionless — anyone can run a relayer to observe source chains for ICM messages and deliver them to destination chains. Relayers earn fees deposited by message senders as their incentive. This open design prevents centralization of the message delivery layer and ensures availability, though dApps can optionally restrict which relayers they accept.

alternates.question: What is the relationship between relayers and validators in Avalanche's cross-chain messaging architecture?
options: Relayers and validators are the same entities performing dual roles
Validators sign messages; relayers transport them
Relayers validate blocks while validators deliver cross-chain messages
Both roles are handled automatically by AvalancheGo without separate operators
alternates.hint: These are separate roles with distinct responsibilities in the message lifecycle.
alternates.explanation: Validators and relayers serve complementary but distinct roles. Validators sign Warp messages using their BLS keys, providing the cryptographic proof of authenticity. Relayers then pick up these signed messages and physically deliver them to the destination chain. A relayer cannot forge messages because it doesn't possess validator private keys, and validators don't handle message transport. This separation ensures security through the validator set and availability through the relayer network.

4023.question: Why might you want to restrict which relayers can deliver messages to your cross-chain application? (Select all that apply)
options: To eliminate all relayers, but practical implementations reveal this is not the case and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
To ensure message delivery quality, prevent spam, guarantee SLAs, or maintain control over the delivery infrastructure for security and reliability
To increase gas costs
To make the system more centralized for no reason
4023.hint: Think about quality of service and security considerations.
4023.explanation: Restricting relayers allows you to ensure message delivery quality and maintain specific service level agreements (SLAs). You might want to guarantee that only relayers you trust or have contracted with can deliver messages to your application. This can help prevent spam, ensure reliable delivery times, maintain security standards, and give you control over the message delivery infrastructure. While AWM is permissionless by default, applications can implement their own restrictions based on their specific reliability and security requirements.

alternates.question: A cross-chain gaming dApp requires guaranteed message delivery within 5 seconds. Why might restricting allowed relayers help achieve this?
options: Restricted relayers have access to faster internet connections by default
Restricting relayers automatically reduces gas costs on destination
Only restricted relayers can sign Warp messages on the source chain
It lets the dApp select relayers committed to specific delivery SLAs
alternates.hint: Think about quality of service guarantees and contractual commitments.
alternates.explanation: By restricting allowed relayers, the dApp can select operators who have committed to specific service level agreements (SLAs), including delivery time guarantees. The dApp can verify relayer performance and replace underperforming operators. Without restrictions, any relayer could deliver messages, and there would be no accountability for meeting time-sensitive requirements.

alternates.question: What are valid reasons for a cross-chain dApp to restrict which relayers can deliver its messages? (Select all that apply)
options: To guarantee delivery SLAs and reliability
To completely eliminate cross-chain messaging overhead
To prevent spam and control delivery security
To increase the gas price on the destination chain
alternates.hint: Think about security, reliability, and quality of service.
alternates.explanation: Restricting relayers allows dApps to ensure delivery quality by selecting trusted operators with proven track records, enforce SLAs for time-sensitive applications, prevent spam from untrusted relayers, and maintain security control over their messaging infrastructure. While AWM is permissionless by default, this optional restriction gives dApps the flexibility to meet specific operational requirements.

4024.question: What is tokenomics and why is it important for L1 blockchains?
options: Tokenomics is about mining cryptocurrency
Tokenomics only refers to creating ERC-20 tokens
Tokenomics is the study of token design, distribution, and utility, crucial for aligning incentives and creating sustainable blockchain economies
Tokenomics only matters for DeFi applications, although some early documentation may have suggested otherwise and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
4024.hint: Think about the economic design of tokens beyond just creating them.
4024.explanation: Tokenomics encompasses the design, distribution, and utility of tokens within a blockchain ecosystem. It's crucial for L1 blockchains because it aligns incentives among participants, drives adoption, and creates sustainable economies. Good tokenomics considers factors like token supply, distribution mechanisms, utility, staking, rewards, and governance. For Avalanche L1s with native tokens, tokenomics determines how the chain's economy functions and how participants are incentivized to maintain and use the network.

alternates.question: Why should an L1 developer care about tokenomics beyond just creating a token?
options: Tokenomics only matters for marketing purposes and branding
Good tokenomics aligns incentives and sustains the chain economy
Tokenomics is purely decorative and has no functional impact on the chain
Token design is automatically handled by the Avalanche protocol itself
alternates.hint: Think about long-term sustainability and participant behavior.
alternates.explanation: Tokenomics goes far beyond token creation — it encompasses the entire economic design of a blockchain. Well-designed tokenomics aligns incentives between validators (who secure the network), users (who pay fees), and developers (who build applications). Poor tokenomics can lead to insufficient validator participation, excessive fees driving users away, or unsustainable emission schedules that devalue the token.

alternates.question: Which aspects of a blockchain's economy does tokenomics encompass? (Select all that apply)
options: Token supply design, distribution mechanisms, and emission schedules
Only the token's name and ticker symbol on exchanges
Utility definition including gas fees, staking, and governance
Only the initial smart contract deployment configuration
alternates.hint: Tokenomics covers the full lifecycle and utility of a token in its ecosystem.
alternates.explanation: Tokenomics is a comprehensive field covering token supply design (fixed vs. inflationary), distribution mechanisms (sales, airdrops, vesting), utility (gas, staking, governance, in-app usage), emission and burn schedules, and how these elements create incentive structures. For L1 blockchains, these decisions fundamentally shape network security, user experience, and long-term economic sustainability.

4025.question: What are the key considerations when designing a token for your L1?
options: Just copying an existing token's design is sufficient, although it might seem logical based on surface-level analysis and involves additional complexity
Only the token name and symbol matter
Token design doesn't affect blockchain success
Supply (fixed or infinite), distribution mechanism, utility (gas, staking, governance), and emission schedule are all critical design considerations
4025.hint: Think about the economic parameters that affect token behavior.
4025.explanation: Token design involves multiple critical considerations: 1) Supply - whether it's fixed, capped, or has infinite emission, 2) Distribution mechanism - how tokens are initially allocated and to whom, 3) Utility - what purposes the token serves (gas fees, staking, governance, etc.), and 4) Emission schedule - how new tokens are released over time. These design choices significantly impact the economic incentives, security model, and long-term sustainability of your L1 blockchain.

alternates.question: An L1 developer is designing their token. Which factor would NOT be a critical design consideration?
options: Whether the supply is fixed or inflationary
The color scheme of the token's logo design
How tokens are distributed to early participants and the community
The emission schedule for new token creation over time
alternates.hint: Focus on economic and functional parameters, not cosmetic ones.
alternates.explanation: Token design requires careful consideration of supply model (fixed, capped, or inflationary), distribution strategy (team allocations, community sales, airdrops), utility definition (gas fees, staking, governance), and emission schedule. While branding matters for marketing, the token logo's color has no impact on the economic mechanics that determine the chain's sustainability and security.

alternates.question: How does choosing between a fixed supply and an inflationary supply model affect an L1's long-term economics?
options: It has no meaningful impact on the chain's economics at all
Inflationary tokens always fail in the long term regardless of design
Fixed supply tokens cannot be used for gas fees on EVM chains
Fixed supply creates scarcity but may lack ongoing validator incentives; inflation funds rewards but dilutes holders
alternates.hint: Consider the tradeoffs between scarcity and ongoing incentive funding.
alternates.explanation: Fixed supply creates scarcity which can support token value, but once initial allocations are distributed, there may be insufficient rewards to incentivize validators long-term unless fees alone are sufficient. Inflationary models can fund perpetual staking rewards and ecosystem grants, but must be carefully balanced to prevent excessive dilution that erodes token value. Many successful L1s use a hybrid approach with controlled inflation.

4026.question: What is a native token in the context of blockchain?
options: Any ERC-20 token deployed on the chain
The blockchain's built-in cryptocurrency used for gas fees and fundamental chain operations, like ETH on Ethereum or AVAX on Avalanche
A token that can only be used for staking, which contradicts the fundamental design principles involved and involves additional complexity
A token created by the blockchain developers
4026.hint: Think about what token is required for basic blockchain operations.
4026.explanation: A native token is the blockchain's built-in cryptocurrency that is fundamental to chain operations. Examples include ETH on Ethereum and AVAX on Avalanche. Native tokens are typically used for paying gas fees for transactions, staking for network security, and other core blockchain functions. They exist at the protocol level rather than being implemented as smart contracts, and every transaction on the blockchain requires the native token for gas fees.

alternates.question: What distinguishes a native token from other tokens deployed on the same blockchain?
options: Native tokens have higher transaction limits than ERC-20 tokens
Native tokens can only be held by registered validator addresses
It is built into the protocol and required for gas
Native tokens are always ERC-20 compliant by default
alternates.hint: Think about what makes a token fundamental to chain operation.
alternates.explanation: A native token exists at the protocol level and is required for gas fee payment on every transaction. Unlike ERC-20 tokens that are smart contracts deployed on top of the chain, the native token is integrated into the blockchain's core code. Every account needs native tokens to pay for any operation, making it the foundational currency of the chain's economy.

alternates.question: Can a blockchain function without a native token?
options: Yes, any ERC-20 token can fully replace the native token
No, EVM chains require a native token for gas processing
Yes, if the chain uses proof of authority consensus instead
No, but only because of regulatory requirements in most jurisdictions
alternates.hint: Consider how the EVM processes transaction fees at the protocol level.
alternates.explanation: EVM-based chains require a native token at the protocol level because gas fee deduction is hardcoded into the execution model. When a transaction is processed, the EVM automatically deducts gas fees in the native token before executing any smart contract logic. While precompiles can enable ERC-20 tokens to pay for gas, there is always an underlying native token at the protocol layer.

4027.question: How do you transfer native tokens compared to ERC-20 tokens on an EVM chain? (Select all that apply)
options: Native tokens are sent using the value field of a transaction, while ERC-20 tokens require calling the transfer function of a smart contract
Both use the same ERC-20 transfer function
ERC-20 tokens are easier to transfer, although it might seem logical based on surface-level analysis and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Native tokens cannot be transferred
4027.hint: Think about the difference between protocol-level tokens and contract tokens.
4027.explanation: Native tokens and ERC-20 tokens are transferred differently. Native tokens are sent by including a value in the value field of a transaction, which is processed at the protocol level. ERC-20 tokens, being smart contracts, require calling the transfer function of the token contract, which then updates balances in the contract's state. This fundamental difference affects how wallets interact with these tokens and how gas fees are calculated.

alternates.question: A developer writes `msg.value` in Solidity to check the amount sent. Does this work for ERC-20 token transfers?
options: No, msg.value only reflects native token amounts
Yes, msg.value works for all token types on EVM chains
Yes, but only for wrapped tokens like wAVAX and wETH
No, msg.value is deprecated in newer Solidity versions
alternates.hint: msg.value is a protocol-level field that predates token standards.
alternates.explanation: The msg.value field only reflects native tokens sent with a transaction — it is a protocol-level concept. ERC-20 transfers happen through smart contract function calls (transfer or transferFrom) that update the contract's internal balance mapping. To check ERC-20 amounts, you must query the token contract's balanceOf function. This fundamental difference illustrates how native tokens and ERC-20 tokens operate at different levels.

alternates.question: Why does sending an ERC-20 token require more gas than sending native tokens? (Select all that apply)
options: ERC-20 transfers involve smart contract execution and storage updates
Network congestion is inherently higher for ERC-20 transactions
Native transfers are simple protocol-level balance updates with minimal computation
ERC-20 tokens use a different consensus mechanism than native tokens
alternates.hint: Consider what happens computationally during each type of transfer.
alternates.explanation: Native token transfers are simple protocol-level operations that update two balances. ERC-20 transfers require executing smart contract code (the transfer function), updating the contract's storage (balance mappings), emitting events, and potentially checking allowances for transferFrom. Each of these operations consumes additional gas, making ERC-20 transfers inherently more expensive than native token transfers.

4028.question: What is the ERC-20 token standard and why is it important?
options: ERC-20 is a type of blockchain consensus
ERC-20 is a programming language, but in reality the system operates quite differently and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
ERC-20 is a standardized interface for fungible tokens on EVM chains, ensuring compatibility and interoperability across wallets and dApps
ERC-20 is only used for stablecoins
4028.explanation: ERC-20 is a widely-adopted token standard on EVM-compatible chains that defines a common interface for fungible tokens. It specifies standard functions like transfer, approve, balanceOf, and transferFrom. This standardization is crucial because it ensures that all ERC-20 tokens work consistently with wallets, exchanges, and dApps. Developers know what functions to expect, and users can interact with any ERC-20 token using the same wallet interface.

alternates.question: Which functions are part of the core ERC-20 standard interface? (Select all that apply)
options: transfer(), approve(), and balanceOf()
stake() and unstake() for validator operations
totalSupply(), transferFrom(), and allowance()
mint() and burn() for supply management
alternates.hint: Think about the core operations for fungible token management.
alternates.explanation: The ERC-20 standard requires six core functions: transfer() for direct sends, approve() for setting allowances, transferFrom() for delegated transfers, balanceOf() for checking balances, totalSupply() for the total token count, and allowance() for checking approved amounts. Functions like mint, burn, stake, and unstake are optional extensions not part of the base standard.

alternates.question: Why do wallet applications like MetaMask seamlessly support thousands of different tokens?
options: MetaMask has custom code for each individual token
Token developers must register tokens with MetaMask before use
All ERC-20 tokens share the same standard interface
MetaMask only supports tokens listed on centralized exchanges
alternates.hint: Think about what standardization means for tool compatibility.
alternates.explanation: The ERC-20 standard defines a uniform interface that all compliant tokens implement. Wallets like MetaMask only need to implement support for the standard functions (transfer, balanceOf, approve, etc.) once, and they automatically work with any ERC-20 token. This interoperability is the primary value of having a token standard — it eliminates the need for custom integration with each individual token.

4029.question: What are the key differences between native tokens and ERC-20 tokens on an L1?
options: ERC-20 tokens are always better than native tokens, but in reality the system operates quite differently and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Native tokens exist at the protocol level for gas fees and staking, while ERC-20 tokens are smart contracts with more flexibility but require gas to transfer
There are no significant differences
Native tokens can only be used on C-Chain
4029.hint: Think about where each type of token exists and what it's used for.
4029.explanation: Native tokens exist at the protocol level and are used for gas fees, staking, and core chain operations. They don't require smart contract interactions for basic transfers. ERC-20 tokens are implemented as smart contracts, offering more flexibility in functionality (custom logic, permissions, etc.) but requiring gas in the native token to perform operations. Native tokens are essential for chain operation, while ERC-20 tokens can represent any asset or utility built on top of the chain.

alternates.question: A developer needs to build a staking contract. Should the staking rewards be paid in the native token or an ERC-20 token?
options: Always native tokens because ERC-20 tokens cannot be used for rewards
Always ERC-20 tokens because they are more secure than native tokens
It depends — native aligns with gas economics; ERC-20 offers flexibility
Neither — staking rewards must be distributed off-chain
alternates.hint: Consider the tradeoffs between protocol-level and contract-level tokens.
alternates.explanation: The choice depends on the L1's design goals. Native token rewards align staking incentives with gas fee economics, creating cohesive tokenomics where validators are rewarded in the same token users spend. ERC-20 rewards offer flexibility — custom logic, multiple reward tokens, and programmatic features. Many L1s use native tokens for staking to maximize economic alignment between network security and token utility.

alternates.question: Which statement accurately describes a limitation of native tokens compared to ERC-20 tokens?
options: Native tokens cannot be transferred between accounts
Native tokens have higher gas fees than ERC-20 tokens
Native tokens lack programmable flexibility of smart contracts
Native tokens cannot be used in DeFi protocols at all
alternates.hint: Think about what smart contracts can do that protocol-level tokens cannot.
alternates.explanation: Native tokens operate at the protocol level with fixed behavior — they can be transferred and used for gas, but lack programmable features. ERC-20 tokens, being smart contracts, can implement custom logic like transfer restrictions, automatic fee distribution, vesting schedules, rebasing mechanisms, and more. This is why DeFi often wraps native tokens into ERC-20 versions to gain this programmability.

4030.question: What are wrapped tokens and why are they used?
options: Wrapped tokens are encrypted versions of regular tokens
Wrapped tokens are only used for cross-chain bridges, despite being a frequently cited but incorrect explanation and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Wrapped tokens are tokenized versions of native assets (like WAVAX for AVAX) that provide ERC-20 interface for DeFi compatibility while maintaining 1:1 value backing
Wrapped tokens are always worth less than native tokens
4030.hint: Think about making native tokens compatible with smart contracts.
4030.explanation: Wrapped tokens are ERC-20 versions of native tokens (like WAVAX for AVAX) that make native assets compatible with smart contracts expecting the ERC-20 interface. They maintain 1:1 backing with the native asset—when you wrap AVAX to WAVAX, your AVAX is held in a contract and you receive an equivalent amount of WAVAX. This is useful for DeFi applications that require the ERC-20 standard. You can unwrap at any time to get your native tokens back.

alternates.question: When a user wraps 100 AVAX into 100 wAVAX, where does the original AVAX go?
options: It is burned and permanently removed from circulation
It remains in the user's wallet alongside the wAVAX tokens
It is locked in the wrapping contract as collateral
It is sent to the Avalanche Foundation treasury
alternates.hint: Wrapped tokens must be fully backed to maintain their 1:1 peg.
alternates.explanation: When wrapping AVAX to wAVAX, the original AVAX is deposited into and held by the wrapping smart contract. This creates a 1:1 backing — the contract holds exactly as much AVAX as there is wAVAX in circulation. When a user unwraps their wAVAX, the smart contract returns the corresponding AVAX and the wAVAX is burned, maintaining the peg at all times.

alternates.question: Why can't native tokens like AVAX be used directly in liquidity pools that require ERC-20 tokens?
options: Native tokens are too valuable for liquidity pool operations
Liquidity pools only accept stablecoins for risk management
Native tokens don't implement the ERC-20 interface that pools require
Native tokens have transfer limits that prevent pool deposits
alternates.hint: Think about the technical interface requirements of DeFi contracts.
alternates.explanation: Liquidity pool contracts are built to interact with the ERC-20 interface — they call functions like transferFrom(), approve(), and balanceOf() on deposited tokens. Native tokens like AVAX exist at the protocol level and don't implement these functions. Wrapped tokens solve this by providing an ERC-20-compliant version that DeFi contracts can interact with using the standard interface.

4031.question: What is a custom native token on an Avalanche L1? (Select all that apply)
options: A token that only works on testnet
A token that cannot be transferred, although some early documentation may have suggested otherwise and involves additional complexity
A token configured as the native gas token of an L1 instead of AVAX, giving the L1 independent tokenomics
A custom ERC-20 token with high gas fees
4031.hint: Think about what token users pay for gas fees.
4031.explanation: A custom native token is a token that serves as the gas token for an Avalanche L1 instead of using AVAX. This gives the L1 independent tokenomics where users pay gas fees in the custom token rather than AVAX. It's configured at the L1 level and allows the chain to have its own economic model separate from the Avalanche C-Chain. This is particularly useful for application-specific chains that want complete control over their token economics.

alternates.question: On an Avalanche L1 configured with a custom native token called GAME, what currency do users pay gas fees in?
options: AVAX, since it is the primary Avalanche token
ETH, since the L1 is EVM-compatible
GAME, the L1's custom native token
Any ERC-20 token of the user's choice
alternates.hint: The native token is used for gas fees by definition.
alternates.explanation: When an L1 is configured with a custom native token, that token replaces AVAX as the gas currency for all operations on that chain. Users pay transaction fees in GAME, validators earn GAME as rewards, and the entire chain economy operates around this custom token. This gives the L1 complete independence over its token economics.

alternates.question: What benefit does an Avalanche L1 gain by using a custom native token instead of AVAX for gas fees? (Select all that apply)
options: Complete control over token supply and emission
Automatic compatibility with Ethereum mainnet dApps
Independent economics not tied to AVAX price volatility
Free transactions for all users on the chain
alternates.hint: Think about economic sovereignty and price independence.
alternates.explanation: A custom native token gives an L1 full sovereignty over its economics: the team controls the supply model, emission rate, and distribution. The chain's gas costs are denominated in the custom token rather than AVAX, insulating users from AVAX price volatility. This is critical for gaming chains, enterprise applications, and any L1 that needs predictable or controlled fee economics.

4032.question: What is the difference between a custom native token and an ERC-20 gas token on an L1?
options: Custom native tokens cannot be transferred
There is no difference, which is an oversimplification of the actual process and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Custom native tokens exist at protocol level and work like ETH on Ethereum, while ERC-20 gas tokens are smart contracts that can be used for gas via precompiles
ERC-20 gas tokens are always better
4032.hint: Think about protocol-level vs. contract-level implementation.
4032.explanation: Custom native tokens are implemented at the protocol level and function like ETH on Ethereum—they are the fundamental currency of the chain. ERC-20 gas tokens are smart contracts that can be configured to pay for gas through precompiles, but they still exist as contracts on top of a base native token. Custom native tokens provide cleaner UX as users don't need a separate token for gas, while ERC-20 gas tokens offer more flexibility but added complexity.

alternates.question: An L1 operator wants gas fees paid in an existing ERC-20 token rather than creating a new native token. How is this typically achieved?
options: By simply setting the token address in the genesis file directly
By forking the EVM to remove native token support entirely
Through precompile configurations that enable ERC-20 gas payment
This is not possible on Avalanche L1s at all
alternates.hint: Think about how contract-level tokens can be integrated with protocol-level gas mechanics.
alternates.explanation: ERC-20 gas tokens are enabled through precompiles that bridge the gap between contract-level tokens and protocol-level gas mechanics. The chain still has an underlying native token at the protocol layer, but the precompile allows users to pay gas fees using a specified ERC-20 token. This approach differs from custom native tokens, which are implemented directly at the protocol level.

alternates.question: What is a key tradeoff of using an ERC-20 gas token versus a custom native token on an L1?
options: Custom native tokens give simpler UX; ERC-20 gas tokens add complexity but reuse existing tokens
ERC-20 gas tokens are always cheaper to use
There are no meaningful differences in practice between the two approaches
ERC-20 gas tokens are inherently more secure than custom native tokens
alternates.hint: Consider user experience and implementation complexity.
alternates.explanation: Custom native tokens work like ETH on Ethereum — users need only one token for everything, creating clean UX. ERC-20 gas tokens let you reuse an existing token for gas but add complexity: there's still an underlying native token at the protocol layer, precompile configuration is needed, and users may encounter edge cases where the base native token surfaces. The choice depends on whether UX simplicity or token reusability is the higher priority.

4033.question: Can the native token of an L1 also serve as its staking token for Proof of Stake validation?
options: Yes, the native token can serve both as the gas token and the staking token, aligning economic incentives
Staking is not possible on Avalanche L1s
Only ERC-20 tokens can be used for staking, which is a common misconception in distributed systems and involves additional complexity
No, staking and gas must use different tokens
4033.hint: Think about economic alignment and token utility.
4033.explanation: Yes, the native token can serve both as the gas token and the staking token for an L1. This creates strong economic alignment—validators must stake the same token that users pay for gas, aligning interests between validators and users. This dual utility increases token demand and creates a more cohesive economic model. However, L1s can also choose to use separate tokens for staking and gas if their specific use case requires it.

alternates.question: An L1 uses its native token CHAIN for both gas fees and PoS staking. What economic benefit does this dual utility create?
options: It reduces the total gas consumption of the network automatically
It aligns validator and user incentives around the same token economy
It eliminates the need for a governance mechanism on the chain
It automatically doubles the token's market price due to demand
alternates.hint: Think about how shared token usage connects different network participants.
alternates.explanation: When the same token is used for gas and staking, validators who secure the network are rewarded in the token that users spend on fees. This creates a direct economic link: validators benefit when network usage (and thus fee revenue) increases, incentivizing them to maintain high-quality service. Users benefit from strong security provided by well-incentivized validators. This alignment strengthens the overall network economy.

alternates.question: Can an Avalanche L1 choose to use separate tokens for gas fees and validator staking?
options: No, the same token must always serve both purposes by protocol requirement
Only if the L1 has fewer than 10 validators registered
Yes, L1s can configure separate tokens based on needs
Only if both tokens are ERC-20 tokens on the C-Chain
alternates.hint: Think about the configurability of Avalanche L1s.
alternates.explanation: Avalanche L1s offer flexibility in token configuration. While using the same token for gas and staking creates economic alignment, some use cases may benefit from separate tokens. For example, an L1 might use a stablecoin for gas (predictable fees) but a separate volatile token for staking (appreciation incentive for validators). The architecture supports both approaches to accommodate diverse application requirements.

4034.question: What are precompiles in the context of Avalanche L1s?
options: Precompiles are the same as regular smart contracts
Precompiles are only used for testing
Precompiles are functions built into the VM at a deeper level than smart contracts, enabling customization and protocol-level features with better performance
Precompiles are compiled smart contracts stored on-chain, although it might seem logical based on surface-level analysis and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
4034.hint: Think about functionality that goes beyond what smart contracts can do.
4034.explanation: Precompiles are functions implemented at the VM level, deeper than smart contracts. They enable protocol-level customization and features that would be impossible or impractical with smart contracts alone. Precompiles can access chain internals, modify native token behavior, implement custom gas logic, and provide better performance than equivalent smart contract implementations. Examples include the Native Minter precompile for minting native tokens and Fee Config precompile for dynamic fee management.

alternates.question: Why can precompiles perform operations that would be impossible with regular smart contracts?
options: Precompiles have access to more ETH than smart contracts do
Precompiles are written in Solidity which is more powerful than EVM bytecode
They execute at the VM level with access to chain internals
Precompiles bypass all security checks and access control
alternates.hint: Think about the level of access each type of code has within the blockchain stack.
alternates.explanation: Precompiles are implemented directly in the virtual machine's code (typically Go for Subnet-EVM), giving them access to chain-level internals like native token minting, fee configuration, and access control lists. Smart contracts run in a sandboxed environment with limited access to the protocol layer. This deeper integration allows precompiles to modify chain behavior that smart contracts cannot influence.

alternates.question: A developer needs to dynamically adjust gas fees on their L1 based on network conditions. Why would they use a precompile rather than a smart contract for this?
options: Smart contracts cannot read any block data from the chain
Fee configuration is protocol-level, only precompiles can modify it
Precompiles are free to execute with zero gas cost
Smart contracts cannot store numeric values in their state
alternates.hint: Gas fee parameters are part of the chain's core configuration, not smart contract state.
alternates.explanation: Gas fee parameters (base fee, block gas limit, target block rate) are protocol-level configurations that the EVM uses when processing every transaction. Smart contracts cannot modify these parameters because they run in a sandboxed execution environment. The Fee Config precompile has the necessary access to modify these chain-level settings, allowing authorized addresses to dynamically tune the chain's fee structure.

4035.question: What are some examples of precompiles available for Avalanche L1 customization? (Select all that apply)
options: Precompiles are not customizable
Native Minter for minting native tokens, Fee Config for dynamic fees, Contract Deployer Allow List for deployment restrictions, and Tx Allow List for transaction permissions
Only ERC-20 related precompiles exist
Only basic math functions, despite appearing correct based on incomplete information and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
4035.hint: Think about chain-level features beyond smart contract capabilities.
4035.explanation: Avalanche offers several powerful precompiles for L1 customization: 1) Native Minter allows authorized contracts to mint native tokens, 2) Fee Config enables dynamic adjustment of gas fees, 3) Contract Deployer Allow List restricts who can deploy contracts, 4) Tx Allow List controls who can submit transactions, and 5) Reward Manager configures block rewards. These precompiles enable protocol-level features that give L1 operators fine-grained control over their chain's behavior and economics.

alternates.question: Which precompile would an L1 operator use to control which addresses can deploy smart contracts on their chain?
options: Native Minter
Fee Config Manager
Reward Manager
Contract Deployer Allow List
alternates.hint: This precompile restricts a specific on-chain action to authorized addresses.
alternates.explanation: The Contract Deployer Allow List precompile restricts smart contract deployment to authorized addresses. This is useful for enterprise L1s, regulated environments, or chains that want to curate their application ecosystem. Only addresses on the allow list can deploy new contracts, while all users can still interact with existing ones.

alternates.question: An enterprise L1 wants to restrict both who can deploy contracts and who can submit transactions. Which precompiles should they configure? (Select all that apply)
options: Contract Deployer Allow List for deployment restrictions
Native Minter for creating new tokens as needed
Tx Allow List for transaction submission restrictions
Reward Manager for configuring validator block rewards
alternates.hint: Think about which precompiles gate deployment and transaction submission respectively.
alternates.explanation: The Contract Deployer Allow List restricts which addresses can deploy new smart contracts, while the Tx Allow List restricts which addresses can submit transactions at all. Together, these precompiles create a permissioned environment suitable for enterprise use cases where both application deployment and user access need to be controlled. Other precompiles like Native Minter and Reward Manager serve different purposes.

4036.question: How do precompiles interact with smart contracts?
options: Only system administrators can call precompiles
Smart contracts must be recompiled to use precompiles, although it might seem logical based on surface-level analysis and involves additional complexity
Precompiles are called from smart contracts using their predefined addresses and interfaces, enabling contracts to access protocol-level functionality
Precompiles cannot interact with smart contracts
4036.hint: Think about the interface between contract-level and protocol-level code.
4036.explanation: Precompiles are called from smart contracts using predefined addresses and Solidity interfaces. Each precompile has a specific address (like 0x0200000000000000000000000000000000000001 for Native Minter) and a defined interface. Smart contracts import these interfaces and call the precompile functions like they would call another contract. This allows smart contracts to access protocol-level functionality while maintaining the security and permissions model defined by the precompile. For example, a staking contract can call the Native Minter precompile to mint rewards.

alternates.question: How does a smart contract invoke the Native Minter precompile to mint new native tokens?
options: By sending a special transaction type to the genesis block
By importing the precompile's source code into the contract
By calling the precompile's interface at its predefined address
By requesting minting through an off-chain API call
alternates.hint: Precompiles are accessed like regular contracts but at specific addresses.
alternates.explanation: Smart contracts interact with precompiles by calling their Solidity interface at a predefined address (e.g., 0x0200000000000000000000000000000000000001 for Native Minter). The contract imports the precompile's interface, creates a reference to the precompile address, and calls functions on it just like it would call another smart contract. The VM intercepts calls to these special addresses and executes the precompile's native code.

alternates.question: A staking contract needs to mint native tokens as rewards. What must be true for this to work with the Native Minter precompile?
options: The staking contract must be deployed before the precompile is activated on the chain
The staking contract must hold a minimum balance of native tokens first
The contract's address must be on the Native Minter's allow list
The staking contract must be owned by a registered validator
alternates.hint: Precompiles use access control to determine who can invoke their functions.
alternates.explanation: The Native Minter precompile uses an AllowList to control which addresses can mint tokens. For a staking contract to mint rewards, its contract address must be added to the AllowList as an Enabled or Admin address. Without this authorization, calling the mint function will revert. This access control prevents unauthorized token creation while allowing designated contracts to programmatically mint tokens as needed.

4037.question: What is the ERC-20 to Native bridge pattern in ICTT?
options: Creating two identical blockchains
Eliminating the need for bridges
Locking an ERC-20 token on the source chain and minting it as the native gas token on the destination L1 using Native Minter precompile
Converting all tokens to Bitcoin, though further analysis shows this reasoning is flawed and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
4037.hint: Think about making an ERC-20 become the gas token of another chain.
4037.explanation: The ERC-20 to Native pattern involves locking an ERC-20 token on the source chain (like C-Chain) and representing it as the native gas token on a destination L1. This uses the Native Minter precompile to mint the native currency. For example, you could lock USDC (an ERC-20) on C-Chain and have it become the native gas token on your L1, so users pay gas in USDC. This requires the destination L1 to have the Native Minter precompile enabled and properly configured.

alternates.question: A developer wants users on their L1 to pay gas fees using a token that exists as an ERC-20 on C-Chain. Which ICTT bridge pattern achieves this?
options: Native to Native bridging between two L1s
ERC-20 to ERC-20 bridging with a wrapper
ERC-20 to Native bridging using the Native Minter precompile
Direct token migration without any bridge
alternates.hint: Think about converting an ERC-20 into a gas token on another chain.
alternates.explanation: The ERC-20 to Native bridge pattern locks an ERC-20 token on the source chain (like C-Chain) and mints it as the native gas token on the destination L1 using the Native Minter precompile. This allows any ERC-20, such as USDC, to become the gas token on a custom L1.

alternates.question: In ERC-20 to Native bridging, what happens to the ERC-20 tokens on the source chain when a user bridges them?
options: They are burned permanently
They are transferred to the user's wallet on the destination
They are locked in the ERC20TokenHome contract as collateral
They are converted to AVAX automatically
alternates.hint: Think about how the 1:1 peg is maintained between chains.
alternates.explanation: When a user bridges ERC-20 tokens to become native tokens, the source tokens are locked (not burned) in the ERC20TokenHome contract as collateral. This ensures a 1:1 backing between locked source tokens and minted native tokens on the destination. The tokens can be unlocked later when someone bridges native tokens back.

4038.question: When creating an L1 for ERC-20 to Native bridging, what precompile must be enabled?
options: The Native Minter precompile must be enabled to allow the TokenRemote contract to mint the native token
No precompiles are needed
All precompiles must be enabled, despite appearing correct based on incomplete information and involves additional complexity
Only the Fee Config precompile
4038.hint: Think about what capability is needed to create native tokens on demand.
4038.explanation: The Native Minter precompile must be enabled when creating an L1 for ERC-20 to Native bridging. This precompile allows authorized addresses (specifically the NativeTokenRemote contract) to mint the native gas token when users bridge tokens from the source chain. During L1 creation, you configure which addresses have minting permissions. Without the Native Minter precompile, the destination chain cannot create native tokens in response to bridge operations.

alternates.question: Without the Native Minter precompile enabled on the destination L1, what would happen when the NativeTokenRemote contract tries to process a bridge message?
options: It would mint ERC-20 tokens as a fallback
The transaction would fail because the contract cannot mint native tokens
The relayer would retry until it succeeds
Tokens would be created but with no value
alternates.hint: Consider what permission the contract needs to create native tokens.
alternates.explanation: Without the Native Minter precompile, the NativeTokenRemote contract has no mechanism to create native tokens on the destination L1. The bridge transaction would fail because minting native tokens requires explicit precompile permissions. This is why enabling the Native Minter and granting permissions to the remote contract is a prerequisite for ERC-20 to Native bridging.

alternates.question: During L1 creation for ERC-20 to Native bridging, which address must be granted Native Minter permissions?
options: The relayer's address
The ERC20TokenHome contract address
The L1 owner's address
The NativeTokenRemote contract address
alternates.hint: Think about which contract actually needs to mint native tokens.
alternates.explanation: The NativeTokenRemote contract is the one that receives bridge messages and mints native tokens on the destination L1. Therefore, it must be granted Native Minter permissions during L1 configuration. Without these permissions, the remote contract cannot create the native tokens users expect to receive when bridging.

4039.question: Why is a relayer necessary for the native token bridge to function? (Select all that apply)
options: To store tokens permanently, but this does not accurately reflect the underlying architecture and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
To validate transactions on both chains
To mint new tokens on the source chain
To observe the source chain for ICM messages and deliver them to the destination chain, enabling the actual cross-chain token movement
4039.hint: Think about the message delivery mechanism.
4039.explanation: A relayer is necessary because it provides the transport layer for cross-chain messages. When tokens are locked on the source chain, an ICM message is created, but this message doesn't automatically appear on the destination chain. The relayer observes the source chain for these messages and physically delivers them to the destination chain, where the remote contract can process them and mint tokens. Without a relayer, messages would be created but never delivered, and the bridge would not function.

alternates.question: If no relayer is running between the source and destination chains, what happens when a user locks tokens in the ERC20TokenHome contract?
options: The tokens are automatically unlocked after a timeout
The ICM message is created but never delivered, so no native tokens are minted
The P-Chain forwards the message instead
The destination chain polls the source for messages
alternates.hint: Think about who physically moves messages between chains.
alternates.explanation: Without a relayer, the ICM message created when tokens are locked on the source chain is never delivered to the destination. The bridge effectively stalls because no entity is watching for messages and submitting them to the other chain. Relayers provide the essential transport layer that makes cross-chain communication work.

alternates.question: What does a relayer do when it detects an ICM message on the source chain?
options: It locks additional tokens as insurance
It validates the message against the P-Chain
It delivers the message to the destination chain, triggering token minting
It broadcasts the message to all Avalanche chains
alternates.hint: Consider the relayer's role in the message delivery pipeline.
alternates.explanation: When a relayer detects an ICM message on the source chain, it picks up that message and submits it as a transaction on the destination chain. This delivery triggers the remote contract to process the message and mint tokens for the user. The relayer acts as a bridge between chains, ensuring messages reach their intended destination.

4040.question: What is the role of the ERC20TokenHome contract in an ERC-20 to Native bridge?
options: To store user passwords, despite what many developers initially assume about the system and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
To pay validators
To create new ERC-20 tokens
To lock the source ERC-20 tokens as collateral and coordinate with the remote contract to mint native tokens on the destination
4040.hint: Think about what happens to the ERC-20 tokens on the source chain.
4040.explanation: The ERC20TokenHome contract manages the ERC-20 tokens on the source chain by locking them as collateral when users initiate a bridge transaction. It coordinates with the NativeTokenRemote contract on the destination through ICM messages, triggering the minting of equivalent native tokens on the destination L1. It also tracks how many tokens are locked for each remote and handles unlocking when tokens are bridged back.

alternates.question: What two responsibilities does the ERC20TokenHome contract handle? (Select all that apply)
options: Minting new ERC-20 tokens from scratch
Locking ERC-20 tokens as collateral when users bridge
Running consensus on the source chain
Coordinating with the remote contract via ICM messages
alternates.hint: Think about what the home contract does with tokens and how it communicates.
alternates.explanation: ERC20TokenHome has two main responsibilities: locking ERC-20 tokens as collateral when users initiate a bridge transaction, and coordinating with the NativeTokenRemote contract on the destination through ICM messages. It tracks how many tokens are locked for each remote and handles unlocking when tokens are bridged back. It does not mint new ERC-20 tokens.

alternates.question: How does ERC20TokenHome track tokens across multiple destination chains?
options: It deploys a separate contract for each destination
It maintains records of how many tokens are locked for each registered remote
It relies on the P-Chain for accounting
It uses a single global counter for all destinations
alternates.hint: Consider what happens when one home contract serves multiple remote chains.
alternates.explanation: ERC20TokenHome tracks the amount of locked tokens for each registered remote contract separately. This per-remote accounting ensures accurate bookkeeping when the home contract serves multiple destination chains. When tokens are bridged back from a specific remote, the home contract knows exactly how many tokens to unlock for that particular route.

4041.question: What is unique about the NativeTokenRemote contract compared to ERC20TokenRemote?
options: It's easier to deploy
It's more expensive to use
It uses the Native Minter precompile to mint and burn the destination chain's native gas token rather than ERC-20 tokens
It doesn't require registration, although some early documentation may have suggested otherwise and involves additional complexity
4041.hint: Think about what type of token is being created on the destination.
4041.explanation: NativeTokenRemote is unique because it mints and burns the destination chain's native gas token using the Native Minter precompile, rather than minting ERC-20 tokens like ERC20TokenRemote does. This means bridged tokens become the actual native currency that users pay for gas. The contract must be granted Native Minter permissions during L1 setup. It implements the IWrappedNativeToken interface and always uses 18 decimals (standard for native EVM tokens).

alternates.question: Why does NativeTokenRemote always use 18 decimals?
options: 18 decimals is an arbitrary choice with no technical reason
The ERC-20 standard requires exactly 18 decimals
Native EVM tokens always use 18 decimals, which is the standard for wei-based accounting
The Native Minter precompile only supports 18 decimals
alternates.hint: Think about how native token units work in EVM chains.
alternates.explanation: NativeTokenRemote uses 18 decimals because native EVM tokens follow the wei standard, where 1 token equals 10^18 wei. This is a fundamental property of EVM-compatible chains, not a choice made by the contract. Since NativeTokenRemote mints the chain's native gas token, it must conform to this standard.

alternates.question: What interface does NativeTokenRemote implement to handle wrapping and unwrapping of native tokens?
options: IERC721Receiver
IWrappedNativeToken
IERC20Metadata
ITokenBridge
alternates.hint: Think about the interface needed to wrap native tokens into a contract-compatible form.
alternates.explanation: NativeTokenRemote implements the IWrappedNativeToken interface, which provides functionality for wrapping and unwrapping native tokens. This allows the contract to receive native tokens and provide an ERC-20 compatible interface when needed, similar to how WETH works on Ethereum. This dual capability distinguishes it from ERC20TokenRemote.

4042.question: Why must you add collateral to the ERC20TokenHome contract before bridging tokens?
options: To ensure the home contract has tokens available to lock when users bridge, maintaining the 1:1 backing between locked source tokens and minted destination tokens
To pay the developers
Collateral is not actually necessary, but this does not accurately reflect the underlying architecture and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
To increase gas fees
4042.hint: Think about what backs the native tokens minted on the destination.
4042.explanation: Adding collateral to the ERC20TokenHome contract ensures there are tokens available to lock when users initiate bridge transactions. This maintains the fundamental 1:1 backing guarantee—for every native token minted on the destination L1, there must be an equivalent ERC-20 token locked as collateral on the source chain. Without sufficient collateral, bridge operations would fail or the peg would break. Collateral must be approved and transferred to the home contract before bridge operations can begin.

alternates.question: What would happen if someone tries to bridge tokens through ERC20TokenHome without sufficient collateral?
options: The bridge would mint unbacked tokens on the destination
The bridge operation would fail
The relayer would add collateral automatically
The bridge would succeed with a reduced amount
alternates.hint: Think about the requirement for 1:1 backing.
alternates.explanation: Without sufficient collateral, the bridge operation fails because the ERC20TokenHome contract cannot lock the required tokens. The 1:1 backing model is fundamental to bridge security, ensuring every minted native token on the destination has a corresponding locked ERC-20 token on the source. The contract enforces this requirement at the transaction level.

alternates.question: Before adding collateral to ERC20TokenHome, what ERC-20 operation must the user perform first?
options: Transfer tokens to the P-Chain
Approve the ERC20TokenHome contract to spend their tokens
Burn a portion of tokens as a fee
Register their wallet with the bridge
alternates.hint: Think about standard ERC-20 token transfer mechanics.
alternates.explanation: Before adding collateral, the user must approve the ERC20TokenHome contract to spend their ERC-20 tokens. This is the standard ERC-20 approve/transferFrom pattern. The home contract then calls transferFrom to move the approved tokens into itself as collateral, ensuring the 1:1 backing model is maintained for bridge operations.

4043.question: What happens during the complete bridge process when transferring ERC-20 tokens to become native tokens on the destination L1? (Select all that apply)
options: Tokens are destroyed on the source chain
ERC-20 tokens are locked in TokenHome, an ICM message is relayed, and NativeTokenRemote mints equivalent native tokens to the user's address
Tokens are duplicated on both chains
The user must manually move tokens, even though this sounds plausible at first glance and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
4043.hint: Think about the lock, relay, and mint sequence.
4043.explanation: The complete bridge process involves: 1) User approves and calls the ERC20TokenHome contract to lock their ERC-20 tokens as collateral, 2) TokenHome emits an ICM message about the transfer, 3) A relayer picks up this message and delivers it to the destination L1, 4) NativeTokenRemote receives the message and uses the Native Minter precompile to mint equivalent native tokens to the user's address. The bridged tokens are now the native gas token on the destination L1. To bridge back, the process reverses—native tokens are burned and ERC-20s are unlocked.

alternates.question: Place the following steps of the ERC-20 to Native bridge process in order: (1) NativeTokenRemote mints native tokens (2) User approves and locks ERC-20s (3) Relayer delivers ICM message. What is the correct sequence?
alternates.hint: Think about what must happen before each subsequent step can occur.
alternates.explanation: The correct sequence is: (2) User approves and locks ERC-20 tokens in ERC20TokenHome, which creates an ICM message, (3) Relayer picks up the ICM message and delivers it to the destination chain, (1) NativeTokenRemote receives the message and mints native tokens to the user's address. Each step depends on the previous one completing.

alternates.question: How does a user bridge native tokens BACK from the destination L1 to recover their original ERC-20 tokens?
options: They must deploy a new bridge contract in the reverse direction
They burn native tokens on the destination, which triggers the home contract to unlock ERC-20s
They send native tokens to the relayer's address
Reverse bridging is not supported
alternates.hint: Think about the reverse of lock-and-mint.
alternates.explanation: To bridge back, the user burns native tokens on the destination L1 through the NativeTokenRemote contract. This creates an ICM message that the relayer delivers to the source chain. The ERC20TokenHome contract then unlocks the corresponding ERC-20 tokens, returning them to the user. This burn-and-unlock process is the reverse of the lock-and-mint flow.

4044.question: What is the Native to ERC-20 bridge pattern?
options: Creating new blockchains automatically, although some early documentation may have suggested otherwise and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Locking native tokens on the source L1 and minting them as ERC-20 tokens on the destination chain, reversing the ERC-20 to Native pattern
Converting Bitcoin to Ethereum
Mining cryptocurrency
4044.hint: Think about the opposite direction from ERC-20 to Native.
4044.explanation: The Native to ERC-20 pattern is the reverse of ERC-20 to Native bridging. It involves locking the native gas token on a source L1 and minting wrapped versions as ERC-20 tokens on the destination chain (often C-Chain or another L1). For example, if your L1 has a custom native token, you can bridge it to C-Chain as an ERC-20. This makes the native token of your L1 tradeable and usable in DeFi applications on other chains. The NativeTokenHome contract manages locking, while ERC20TokenRemote handles minting.

alternates.question: An L1 has a custom native gas token and the team wants it tradeable on C-Chain DEXes. Which bridge pattern should they use?
options: ERC-20 to Native bridging
Native to ERC-20 bridging
Native to Native bridging
No bridge is needed for DEX trading
alternates.hint: Think about which direction converts a native token into a tradeable ERC-20.
alternates.explanation: Native to ERC-20 bridging locks native tokens on the source L1 and mints wrapped ERC-20 tokens on the destination chain (such as C-Chain). This makes the L1's native token compatible with the ERC-20 ecosystem, enabling trading on DEXes, use in DeFi protocols, and integration with wallets that expect ERC-20 tokens.

alternates.question: Which contracts are deployed on which chains in the Native to ERC-20 pattern?
options: NativeTokenHome on the destination, ERC20TokenRemote on the source
NativeTokenHome on the source L1, ERC20TokenRemote on the destination chain
Both contracts are deployed on the source L1
Both contracts are deployed on the destination chain
alternates.hint: Think about where native tokens are locked and where ERC-20s are minted.
alternates.explanation: In the Native to ERC-20 pattern, NativeTokenHome is deployed on the source L1 where native tokens are locked, and ERC20TokenRemote is deployed on the destination chain where wrapped ERC-20 tokens are minted. This separation ensures each contract operates on the chain where its token type exists.

4045.question: Why deploy a wrapped ERC-20 token contract on the destination chain in Native to ERC-20 bridging?
options: To make transactions faster
To provide the ERC-20 representation that will be minted when users bridge native tokens from the source L1
Wrapped tokens are not needed
To pay validators, although some early documentation may have suggested otherwise and involves additional complexity
4045.hint: Think about what token standard users need on the destination.
4045.explanation: Deploying a wrapped ERC-20 token contract on the destination chain provides the token that will represent the source L1's native token. When users bridge native tokens from the source L1, the ERC20TokenRemote contract mints these wrapped ERC-20 tokens on the destination. This makes the source L1's native token compatible with the ERC-20 ecosystem, allowing it to be used in DeFi, traded on DEXes, or integrated with wallets that expect ERC-20 tokens. Users can later burn these wrapped tokens to redeem their native tokens on the source L1.

alternates.question: What would users receive on the destination chain without a wrapped ERC-20 token contract deployed?
options: Native tokens of the destination chain
Nothing, because there is no contract to mint the token representation
AVAX tokens as a default fallback
Unwrapped raw token data
alternates.hint: Think about what creates the token on the destination side.
alternates.explanation: Without a wrapped ERC-20 token contract (ERC20TokenRemote) deployed on the destination, there would be no mechanism to create a token representation when native tokens are locked on the source L1. The bridge requires both the home contract for locking and the remote contract for minting to function.

alternates.question: What can users do with wrapped ERC-20 tokens received from a Native to ERC-20 bridge? (Select all that apply)
options: Trade them on DEXes on the destination chain
Use them as collateral in DeFi protocols
Convert them back to native tokens on the source L1
Use them to pay gas on the destination chain
alternates.hint: Think about what ERC-20 tokens enable and how the bridge works bidirectionally.
alternates.explanation: Wrapped ERC-20 tokens from the bridge can be traded on DEXes and used in any application that supports ERC-20 tokens on the destination chain. Users can also burn these wrapped tokens to redeem their original native tokens on the source L1 through the reverse bridge flow. However, they cannot be used for gas on the destination chain since they are ERC-20 tokens, not native tokens.
